# Common Architecture Anti‑Patterns in Solo AI/LLM Projects (and What to Do Instead)

<!-- Copilot-Researcher-Visualization -->

![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Each anti-pattern below outlines **when it tends to appear, the flawed reasoning behind the over-engineered solution, what a simpler alternative looks like, and specific technologies to avoid or prefer.** This reference is intended to help experienced engineers (who may be used to large-scale systems) avoid **“big-company”** overengineering in small-scale AI applications.

***

### 1. **God-Multi-Agent Syndrome** – *One Agent to Rule Them All (Badly)*

*   **When it appears (Trigger):** You’ve built an AI application with a large language model and start adding more and more capabilities: web search, database queries, code generation, emailing, all in one agent. You’ve read about multi-agent systems and think more agents or one “super agent” will solve complex tasks. Maybe you saw demos of AutoGPT or agent frameworks and feel your app needs a swarm of AI agents collaborating to be cutting-edge. The context often is a single LLM already struggling with a long prompt, but you’re tempted to pile on more tools or additional autonomous agents.

*   **What the developer thinks they need (Overengineered Choice):** An elaborate **multi-agent orchestration** or an all-powerful *“God agent.”* This might involve using frameworks like **LangChain’s Agents**, **AutoGPT/Generative Agents**, or setting up multiple LLMs that converse with each other. The developer might create a huge agent prompt with a dozen tools (database search, API calls, calculators, web browsing, etc.) or spawn separate sub-agents for different tasks, thinking this will make the system more capable or scalable. For example, using an “agent orchestration” library to manage multiple AI agents (one for planning, one for execution, one for review, etc.) or adding 20 tools to a single agent’s prompt. [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/the-god-agent-mistake-why-one-mega-agent-always-fails-in-production-1fk1)

*   **What they actually need (Simpler Solution):** **Start with one well-scoped agent or a simple sequential script.** Most small-scale LLM applications do **not** require dynamic multi-agent systems. If your use-case is question-answering or a user assistant, a single agent with a clear prompt and maybe a couple of tool integrations (if truly needed) is enough. Keep the LLM’s scope focused: if it’s a coding assistant, give it coding tools; if it’s a chatbot, maybe allow one or two actions like web search or database query **only if necessary**. Before even adding a second agent, ensure a single agent can’t handle the task by breaking it into steps (you can often implement a sequence of prompt calls or function calls *without* giving the model autonomous control over calling multiple agents). In many cases, a **deterministic script with multiple LLM API calls in sequence** (where you orchestrate the logic) is far easier to test and more reliable than an autonomous multi-agent free-for-all. If you think you need multiple specialized behaviors, consider a *router pattern* (one small classifier model or simple logic that directs queries to different single-purpose prompts/agents) rather than agents invoking agents. Limit the tool count: research shows LLM accuracy degrades with too many tools beyond \~15 choices, and real-world experience shows that giving an agent 20 tools leads to confusion and errors. **Less is more**: constrain the problem for your agent. [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/multi-agent-orchestration-a-guide-to-patterns-that-work-1h81), [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/multi-agent-orchestration-a-guide-to-patterns-that-work-1h81) [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/the-god-agent-mistake-why-one-mega-agent-always-fails-in-production-1fk1), [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/the-god-agent-mistake-why-one-mega-agent-always-fails-in-production-1fk1)

*   **Why the complex choice hurts:** Orchestrating multiple agents (or one mega-agent with endless tools) introduces **huge complexity and unpredictable behavior**. You can end up with “agent loops” where agents call each other or themselves ad infinitum, wasting tokens and money. A single agent loaded with too many tools often becomes unreliable – it can lose track of instructions as the context window overflows, forget what tools to use (the *“lost in the middle”* effect), or use the wrong tool due to the sheer number of options (tool confusion). Debugging these issues is extremely hard: you’re essentially trying to trace *why* an AI made a decision across multiple steps or multiple agents, which can feel like a black box. For a solo developer without a team of ML engineers, this is a nightmare. You’ll spend days or weeks tuning prompts, adding guardrails, and handling edge cases from an over-complicated setup. Meanwhile, a simpler single-agent or scripted approach might have worked with a fraction of the effort and with far more predictability. Remember that each additional agent or tool massively expands the state space you need to reason about. As one guide on multi-agent patterns bluntly advises: “If none of \[the clear justifications for multi-agent] apply, **stay single-agent**… These are cheaper, simpler fixes than introducing agent coordination.” In other words, don’t jump to multi-agent unless you’re **hitting real limits** of single-agent approaches. [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/the-god-agent-mistake-why-one-mega-agent-always-fails-in-production-1fk1) [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/the-god-agent-mistake-why-one-mega-agent-always-fails-in-production-1fk1), [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/the-god-agent-mistake-why-one-mega-agent-always-fails-in-production-1fk1) [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/multi-agent-orchestration-a-guide-to-patterns-that-work-1h81), [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/multi-agent-orchestration-a-guide-to-patterns-that-work-1h81)

*   **Technologies to Avoid at This Scale:** Avoid using frameworks like \**LangChain’s multi-agent* capabilities or **AutoGPT**-style setups for initial prototypes. Don’t use open-source “agent orchestration” libraries (e.g., **Microsoft Autogen**, **Hugging Face Transformers Agents**) until you’ve validated a clear need. Steer clear of complex platforms like **IBM’s Watson Orchestrate** or enterprise AI workflow systems for now. In general, avoid trying to build an “AI that does everything” – break problems into manageable pieces.

*   **Technologies Recommended Instead:** Use a single **LLM API** (OpenAI, Azure, Anthropic, etc.) with carefully designed prompts for each required task. If you have multiple distinct tasks, use simple **conditional logic or a router function** in your code to choose the right prompt or tool rather than giving that responsibility entirely to an LLM. For tool usage, prefer native function calls or API calls directly from your code (many LLMs now support function calling natively, letting you stay in control). For sequences of actions, write a Python script or use lightweight workflow logic (even just a loop or some `if/then` decisions in code) – this keeps you in the driver’s seat. Only consider multi-agent frameworks when single-agent solutions demonstrably fail due to well-understood reasons (e.g., context length limits that can only be overcome by decomposition into multiple agents, or truly independent tasks that must run in parallel). Even then, start with minimal agents and simple communication channels (maybe one planner and a couple of doers). Keep it as straightforward as possible. [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/multi-agent-orchestration-a-guide-to-patterns-that-work-1h81)

### 2. **Vector DB at 100 Items** – *Adopting a Distributed Vector Database far too early.*

*   **When it appears (Trigger):** You’re implementing a **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** pipeline or semantic search for your app. You have a modest set of documents or data (perhaps a few hundred PDF pages or a few thousand embeddings), but you’ve heard that “you need a vector database” to do similarity search for LLM context. There’s a lot of buzz around specialized vector databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus, etc.), so you figure you should integrate one right from the start – maybe an AI helper or tutorial even auto-generates some code for Pinecone or FAISS. Essentially, the anti-pattern is triggered by assuming any use of embeddings must come with a standalone vector search service.

*   **What the developer thinks they need (Overengineered Choice):** Spinning up a **full-blown vector database** or distributed index from day one. This could be choosing a managed service like **Pinecone or Weaviate** or deploying your own cluster of Milvus, ElasticSearch with vector capabilities, or Vespa. The rationale is usually “we might eventually have a lot of data, so let’s prepare for scale now,” or simply not realizing that smaller options exist. For example, using Pinecone with replication and multi-node setup to index a few thousand embeddings, or launching a Docker container for Milvus for a simple personal project. In some cases, developers also create overly complex pipelines feeding data into the vector DB (with message queues and separate services) because that’s how they’ve seen it done at large companies.

*   **What they actually need (Simpler Solution):** **Start with a lightweight, local vector store.** If your data is small (say, under 100k vectors, which covers a *lot* of prototypes and internal tools), you can avoid dedicated external databases entirely. Good alternatives include: [\[mpurayil.com\]](https://mpurayil.com/blog/vector-database-architecture-best-practices-startup)
    *   Use an extension like **pgvector** with PostgreSQL (or the equivalent in SQLite) to add vector search to a traditional SQL database you might already be using. This way your embeddings live alongside your other data, and you get easy queries with no new services to manage. [\[mpurayil.com\]](https://mpurayil.com/blog/vector-database-architecture-best-practices-startup)
    *   For pure in-memory search on small data, use **FAISS** (a C++/Python library by Facebook) or **Annoy**. These libraries let you index and search vectors efficiently in-code. If your data fits in memory (which a few thousand embeddings do easily), this will be super fast.
    *   Even a simple **Python list of embeddings plus a library function** (scikit-learn’s NearestNeighbors or SciPy) can work for very small sets.
        The key is: use the simplest thing that can possibly work. Only when you start pushing the limits (e.g., millions of vectors, or you need multi-user concurrent access with high QPS) should you consider a dedicated vector DB. The CTO of one AI startup advises: *“Unless you have a specific reason to run a dedicated vector database, start with pgvector in Postgres… Good enough performance – sub-50ms queries up to 1M vectors on a decent instance.”* In other words, existing tools can take you quite far. [\[mpurayil.com\]](https://mpurayil.com/blog/vector-database-architecture-best-practices-startup)

*   **Why the complex choice hurts:** Using a cloud vector DB or self-managed cluster too early **slows you down and drains resources**. Managed services like Pinecone can be expensive (often `$$$` from day one for any significant size or throughput), which is usually unwarranted if you don’t have many users or data yet. Self-hosting a vector DB (Milvus/Elasticsearch + vector plugin) brings operational overhead – you’re now worrying about cluster setup, indices, scaling, and maintenance, instead of improving your core application. More moving parts means more things that can break. If something goes wrong with your vector search results, is it a bug in your code, or an obscure issue in the distributed index? Hard to tell without becoming an expert in that vector DB. Also, for small data sizes, these systems can actually be **slower** than an in-memory search due to network calls and distributed coordination. Remember that a vector DB is essentially an optimization for large-scale data; at small scale, it’s like hiring a full construction crew to hang a picture frame. One detailed guide on vector databases for startups flat out says: **“Running a dedicated vector database before 100K vectors” is an anti-pattern – the ops overhead isn’t worth it.** The recommended approach is to postpone that complexity until you really need it. [\[productbuilder.net\]](https://www.productbuilder.net/learn/comparisons/pinecone-vs-faiss), [\[productbuilder.net\]](https://www.productbuilder.net/learn/comparisons/pinecone-vs-faiss) [\[mpurayil.com\]](https://mpurayil.com/blog/vector-database-architecture-best-practices-startup), [\[mpurayil.com\]](https://mpurayil.com/blog/vector-database-architecture-best-practices-startup)

*   **Technologies to Avoid at This Scale:** Avoid deploying **Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus, ElasticSearch with vector search**, or **AWS OpenSearch** for small-scale use cases. Don’t introduce heavy dependencies like **Apache Spark** or **Hadoop** just to process or index a few thousand embeddings. And be wary of blindly following library defaults – for instance, some popular LLM tooling libraries might default to using a cloud vector DB; double-check if it’s truly necessary.

*   **Technologies Recommended Instead:** Use **pgvector with Postgres** (which you might already have for your app’s data) or **SQLite + an embedding extension** for persistence with minimal overhead. For in-memory or file-based local solutions, consider **FAISS** or **Annoy** – these require zero infrastructure and are highly optimized for similarity search on a single machine. There are also simple hosted alternatives like **Chroma** (which can run locally in a lightweight way). If you’re comfortable with Python, libraries like **LlamaIndex** or **LangChain** can use local embeddings with FAISS or even just in-memory lists. The bottom line is to leverage what you have: if your data fits in RAM or a single database, ride that until you truly outgrow it (which might never happen for many internal tools or MVPs). Remember the advice: *prove the need for scale before adding complexity*. [\[mpurayil.com\]](https://mpurayil.com/blog/vector-database-architecture-best-practices-startup)

### 3. **Microservice-itis** – *Fragmenting a small system into microservices*

*   **When it appears (Trigger):** You’re starting an AI app (perhaps a simple web app that calls an LLM and a database) but you come from a microservices background or have seen all the flashy architecture diagrams from big tech. It feels “professional” to split everything into services: maybe an auth service, a vector DB service, a model-serving service, a frontend service, etc. You anticipate future features and think each might be a separate service. If you used an AI code generator, it might even output a scaffold with multiple Docker services by default. This anti-pattern often arises from applying large-scale patterns in a context where a single developer is managing the whole project.

*   **What the developer thinks they need (Overengineered Choice):** A fully **microservices-based architecture** from day one. This means multiple deployable services or functions, each with their own API, often containerized and talking to each other over a network. The developer envisions clear separations like “one service for user management, one for embeddings, one for LLM queries, one for the UI” etc. They might set up an API gateway or use cloud functions for each endpoint. Some even consider a service mesh (e.g., Istio) for managing these services, or separate databases for each service (a common microservice mantra). The thinking is that this will make the system more scalable and maintainable, and allow working on parts independently.

*   **What they actually need (Simpler Solution):** In almost all cases for a small team or solo project, you’ll move faster and have fewer issues with a **modular monolith** – a single service/application that contains all the necessary functionality in one codebase. This doesn’t mean your code should be a tangled mess; you can and should structure it into clear modules or layers internally. But it **runs as one deployable unit**, which simplifies deployment and orchestration drastically. You can use feature flags or configurations to turn components on/off, rather than deploying separate services. A well-structured monolith can later be split *if needed* – but chances are, for an AI MVP or internal tool, that need won’t arise until you have a real, proven scaling or team-size challenge. As Martin Fowler famously advised, *“you shouldn’t start a new project with microservices, even if you’re sure your application will be big enough… almost all successful microservice stories have started with a monolith that got too big and was broken up.”* Until you *feel* that pain, stick to one service. If you truly feel the need for some separation, consider a **modular monolith approach** or at most a **couple of coarse-grained services** (e.g., maybe the front-end is separate from the back-end API, which is common and fine). But don’t slice and dice further without cause. [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html), [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html) [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html)

*   **Why the complex choice hurts:** Adopting microservices prematurely is adding a “**Microservice Premium**” in overhead that you don’t get payback on in a tiny system. Each service might be simpler in isolation, but *you* now have to manage the complexity of distributed systems: inter-service communication, API compatibility, deployment coordination, load balancing, and failure handling across network boundaries. For a solo dev, that’s a tremendous cognitive load. You’ll spend time writing Dockerfiles, config files for each service, setting up communication (or abusing something like Kubernetes just to connect them), and debugging issues like CORS, network timeouts, or inconsistent data models across services. Meanwhile, a monolith avoids virtually all those issues – a function call or library import replaces all that networking and orchestration complexity. Another pain point is that microservices often lead to a **“distributed monolith”** in practice, where everything still depends on each other (e.g., you can’t deploy your “vector service” without also updating your “LLM service” because of shared contracts), thus giving you *the worst of both worlds* – high complexity with none of the supposed agility. And if you’re the only developer, the “multiple teams can develop independently” advantage of microservices is moot. In short, you pay a huge tax in speed and simplicity up front for a benefit (independent scaling and deployment of components) that you likely won’t need until much later, if ever. As one observer noted, even in 2026 microservices remain over-recommended, and many teams *“slow down massively or fail by moving to microservices too early.”* [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html) [\[garnetgrid.com\]](https://www.garnetgrid.com/insights/microservices-anti-patterns) [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html), [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html)

*   **Technologies to Avoid at This Scale:** Don’t start containerizing each little part of your app into separate images with **Docker Compose** or deploying to **AWS Lambda** functions for each endpoint – that can be a maintenance headache. Avoid service mesh tech (Istio, Linkerd), API gateways, or complex messaging systems between services (Kafka, RabbitMQ – more on that below) when a function call would do. Specifically, **skip the whole CNCF litany** (Kubernetes, gRPC microservices, etc.) on day one. If your “architecture diagram” has dozens of boxes for a two-person project, consider it a red flag.

*   **Technologies Recommended Instead:** Use a single framework that can cover most of your needs in one process. For instance, a Python **FastAPI** or **Flask** app can handle web requests, call the LLM API, query a database, and even run background tasks (via threads or Celery) all within one codebase. Tools like **Django** or **Rails** are monolithic by nature (and you can integrate any AI calls into them). If you need a separate front-end, a simple React or Next.js app can serve the UI while the back-end remains monolithic. If you’re set on cloud functions, consider using **one function (or minimal number)** that does multiple steps rather than orchestrating many small ones. Always ask: “Can I implement, test, and deploy this as one service?” – if yes, do that. Use libraries to structure your code into services *within* the app (for example, logical separation of concerns), rather than physically separate processes. This gives you clarity without the hassle of microservice ops.

### 4. **Kubernetes on Day 0** – *Managing a cluster for a one-node app*

*   **When it appears (Trigger):** You want to deploy your LLM app or service and recall that “all modern infrastructure is containerized and orchestrated.” Perhaps you have experience with Docker/Kubernetes from your day job, or online examples show how to package your app into containers and helm charts. You might plan for scaling (“if we get lots of users, we can just add pods”). For a solo dev or small team, the allure of Kubernetes often comes from wanting to apply best practices you’ve seen elsewhere, or thinking it will simplify cloud deployment.

*   **What the developer thinks they need (Overengineered Choice):** Containerize the app (often okay) and then use **Kubernetes (K8s)** to deploy it. This could involve creating a cluster on a cloud provider (EKS on AWS, GKE on Google, etc.) or even running minikube/kind locally. They might also write **Helm charts** or use complex manifest files, set up **CI/CD pipelines** to deploy to the cluster, and configure autoscaling, even if the current load is minimal. In extreme cases, adding a **service mesh (Istio)** because that’s what “modern architectures” do, or using Kubernetes Operators/CRDs for managing the AI model serving. Essentially, treating a simple app as if it were a large, distributed system that justifies a full container orchestration layer.

*   **What they actually need (Simpler Solution):** **A single server or Platform-as-a-Service deployment.** If you’re handling low to moderate traffic (which is likely until you have real traction), a single VM or a simple PaaS can handle it. For example, deploy your container (or just your code) directly to a service like **Heroku, Fly.io, Render, or Vercel** – these abstract away the servers and scaling without you running a cluster. Or manually run your app on a single cloud VM (an EC2, Droplet, etc.) using a process manager (PM2 for Node, Gunicorn for Python, etc.). If you need scalability, you can often vertically scale that one machine to a certain point or use a couple of VMs behind a simple load balancer. Managed container services like **AWS ECS/Fargate or Cloud Run** are also easier than a full K8s cluster. Essentially, use the simplest hosting that **gets you to production**. Only consider K8s when you have multiple services or a need for complex orchestration that clearly can’t be met with simpler tech. Many successful small-scale systems never need to move off simple setups – don’t assume you’ll be the next Twitter overnight.

*   **Why the complex choice hurts:** Kubernetes is **notorious for its complexity** and steep learning curve. It was built to manage Google-scale deployments; using it for a tiny app means you’re dragging in a **huge ops burden** for little gain. Solo devs routinely get bogged down in YAML configuration, helm charts, ingress rules, and service definitions, rather than coding features. One 2025 analysis pointed out that for *“95% of projects out there, \[Docker +] Kubernetes are absolute overkill… Most teams don’t need the complexity, overhead, or steep learning curve these tools bring.”* In a small project, you typically don’t need features like automated pod rescheduling, service discovery, or advanced autoscaling – but you’ll still pay the price of setting them up. Every hour spent debugging why your pod won’t start is an hour not spent improving your LLM prompts or your UI. Aside from time, there’s monetary cost: a minimal K8s cluster often runs several VMs just to keep itself alive (masters, etc.), and extends your deployment times (build container, push image, roll out to cluster). If something breaks in production, you now have multiple layers (your app *and* the cluster) to investigate. As one engineer quipped, using K8s too early is like *“using a sledgehammer to crack a nut – powerful, but you’ll smash a lot of things in the process.”* Unless your application truly demands it, you’re better off avoiding that pain. In fact, even in 2026, writers note that a huge portion of small companies would be better off without K8s – *“Kubernetes is overkill for 80%+ of projects… you don’t need K8s, you need discipline.”*. The discipline in question is to architect simply for your current needs. [\[blog.stackademic.com\]](https://blog.stackademic.com/why-kubernetes-and-docker-are-overkill-for-95-of-projects-out-there-d694f5593749), [\[blog.stackademic.com\]](https://blog.stackademic.com/why-kubernetes-and-docker-are-overkill-for-95-of-projects-out-there-d694f5593749)

*   **Technologies to Avoid at This Scale:** Avoid setting up a **Kubernetes cluster** (EKS, GKE, AKS, or on-prem) for projects with minimal traffic or simple topology. Don’t use heavy container orchestration add-ons like **Istio, Linkerd, Consul, Vault** – these make sense when you have dozens of services and teams, not for one or two services you control. Be cautious with using **Terraform** to manage a full-blown cloud infrastructure for trivial deployments – IaC is powerful, but the overhead might not be worth it if your entire stack is a couple of VMs or a single Docker container.

*   **Technologies Recommended Instead:** Use a **PaaS** like Heroku, Render, Fly.io, Google Cloud Run, or **Vercel** (for frontends) to get your app online with minimal fuss. If you want to stick with containers but not full K8s, consider **Docker Compose** on a single server or a lightweight orchestrator like **Docker Swarm** (much simpler than K8s). Or forego containers entirely and just run your app directly on a VM – for instance, use Ansible or a simple script to set up a Ubuntu server with your web app and a systemd service. Use a **managed database service** instead of hosting your own on K8s. Basically, leverage hosted simplicity: if you’re on AWS, something like **Elastic Beanstalk or Lambda** can run a small app without you managing servers. Always remember: the goal is to maximize your focus on the application, not the infrastructure. You can revisit orchestration once user demand outgrows what you have.

### 5. **“Use Every LLM Framework”** – *Over-complicating prompt calls with heavy orchestration frameworks.*

*   **When it appears (Trigger):** You’re building an LLM-powered app (chatbot, AI assistant, etc.) and you come across popular frameworks like **LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack, PipelineAI** or others. There’s a deluge of blogs, GitHub repos, and tutorials using these to handle everything from prompt creation to tool use and chaining model calls. As an experienced engineer, you might also be tempted to create elaborate abstractions (OOP classes for prompts, generic pipelines for request handling, etc.) to mirror patterns you know from other domains. Essentially, you feel that using or building a framework will make your LLM integration more robust or elegant.

*   **What the developer thinks they need (Overengineered Choice):** Adopting a **full LLM orchestration framework or custom wrapper** around model calls. For example, pulling in LangChain and using its every feature – even if your use case is just one or two prompts – because it seems like the “standard.” Or writing your own generalized “LLM manager” classes to handle prompts, even though a simple function call would do. Some will incorporate **multiple frameworks** (e.g., LangChain *plus* an agent framework, *plus* a prompt templating library) hoping to cover all bases. The developer envisions their app as fully abstracted: swap out LLMs easily, instrument every call, support any chain imaginable. In reality, they might only need a straightforward call to `openai.ChatCompletion.create()` or a couple of steps of logic, but the code is now littered with abstract layers that mirror the complexity of a large-scale system.

*   **What they actually need (Simpler Solution):** **Just call the API (with minimal glue code).** Write clean, direct code for your specific use case instead of introducing extra abstraction layers. If you need to chain a couple of calls, do it with normal code (call the first LLM API, get result, feed into next call). If you need to manage prompts, treat them as templates (maybe store them in files or use simple string formatting). Many developers report that a small amount of custom code outperforms their earlier attempts with heavy frameworks, in both clarity and performance. Only consider a framework if it clearly saves you substantial time *and* you’ve identified that your problem aligns with what it provides. Most often, initial LLM apps don’t need the generality of LangChain or others (which were designed to handle many edge cases in one-size-fits-all fashion). Keep things as **idiomatic and simple** as possible: for instance, use the official SDKs (OpenAI’s Python library, etc.) or simple HTTP calls. If you require state management (conversation memory) or tool usage, see if you can implement a lightweight version yourself – you’ll understand it better and tailor it to your needs. A telling statistic: one developer compared LangChain with a custom approach and found the memory overhead and latency to be significantly higher in LangChain for small-scale use cases, because it pulled in many dependencies and layers that weren’t strictly necessary. [\[mirascope.com\]](https://mirascope.com/blog/langchain-sucks), [\[mirascope.com\]](https://mirascope.com/blog/langchain-sucks) [\[shashankgu...medium.com\]](https://shashankguda.medium.com/challenges-criticisms-of-langchain-b26afcef94e7), [\[shashankgu...medium.com\]](https://shashankguda.medium.com/challenges-criticisms-of-langchain-b26afcef94e7)

*   **Why the complex choice hurts:** These frameworks introduce **dependency bloat and abstraction overhead** that often yield diminishing returns for small projects. For example, LangChain can bring in over 50+ package dependencies (various integrations you might not even use) which can lead to version conflicts and larger memory footprint. More importantly, frameworks like this have a learning curve – you must understand *their* abstractions (Chains, Agents, Memory, etc. in LangChain’s case) and adapt your thinking to their way of doing things. This can slow down development; instead of iterating on your prompt or logic, you’re fighting library nuances. There’s also a stability factor: rapidly evolving LLM frameworks have been known to introduce breaking changes, causing maintenance headaches. If you’re a lone developer, chasing library updates or debugging through multiple layers of library code is time you can’t afford. A Medium critique of LangChain noted that for small projects it *“added unnecessary complexity”* and that developers spent more time debugging the framework than building features. In contrast, by writing a bit of glue code yourself, you remain in full control. You can optimize or tweak anything because you understand every part of the stack. And when something goes wrong, the call stack is your code, not a maze of framework internals. In short, heavy LLM frameworks are often **overkill** (designed for many edge cases and larger teams) – a solo dev should leverage the power of modern LLM APIs directly before abstracting it away. [\[shashankgu...medium.com\]](https://shashankguda.medium.com/challenges-criticisms-of-langchain-b26afcef94e7), [\[shashankgu...medium.com\]](https://shashankguda.medium.com/challenges-criticisms-of-langchain-b26afcef94e7) [\[shashankgu...medium.com\]](https://shashankguda.medium.com/challenges-criticisms-of-langchain-b26afcef94e7)

*   **Technologies to Avoid at This Scale:** Be cautious about jumping on **LangChain** (especially with agents and chains) for simple tasks; avoid it if your use case is straightforward Q\&A, single-step prompts, etc. Don’t use **Haystack** or **Rasa** if a few API calls with some logic can do the job. Avoid trying to use every new “LLMOps” tool by default (e.g., prompt versioning servers, vector stores, model hubs) unless you have a concrete issue they solve. And certainly do not write your **own full framework** in an attempt to generalize your LLM usage – you likely don’t need a custom DSL or complex class hierarchy for prompt handling in a small app.

*   **Technologies Recommended Instead:** Use the **official SDKs** or straightforward API calls. For OpenAI, the `openai` Python library or Node SDK; for open-source models, a minimal wrapper like huggingface’s `transformers` pipeline or the model’s own API. For prompt management, consider simple templating libraries (Jinja2 for Python, etc.) or even just f-strings – whatever makes plain code clear. To manage chains of calls, a few functions in your code may suffice. If you need to manage experimentations with prompts or models, use simple version control (Git) to track changes, or small scripts to run batch evaluations – you might not need a specialized tool yet. The emphasis is on **transparency and control**: you’ll move faster when you can see exactly what’s happening.

### 6. **Full MLOps Pipeline for a Prompt** – *Treating prompt iterations like a production ML pipeline*

*   **When it appears (Trigger):** Coming from a machine learning background or enterprise AI team, you recall all the tools and processes used to manage models: MLflow for experiment tracking, Kubeflow or Airflow for pipelines, CI/CD for model deployment, monitoring for drift, etc. Now you’re doing an LLM project that mostly involves calling an API or at most fine-tuning a model, yet you feel the need to set up similar infrastructure. Perhaps you think: “We need experiment tracking for our prompt engineering” or “Let’s set up a full CI pipeline for our LLM prompts and model retraining.” In essence, you’re applying heavyweight **MLOps** practices to a lightweight problem.

*   **What the developer thinks they need (Overengineered Choice):** A **complete MLOps stack** from the start. This might include:
    *   Setting up **MLflow or Kubeflow Pipelines** to track prompt versions or manage a retraining pipeline.
    *   Using a continuous training pipeline tool like **Vertex AI Pipelines, SageMaker pipelines, or Jenkins/Airflow** to retrain models on a schedule, even if you only have one model that rarely changes.
    *   Implementing a full **CI/CD for machine learning**: unit tests for prompts, automated evaluations with every change, model registry, etc.
    *   Integrating a slew of tools: feature stores (see next anti-pattern), automated model monitoring for concept drift, etc., when your “model” might just be an external API call.
        For example, using MLflow’s new LLM support to log every prompt and response, or deploying your app’s LLM usage behind a custom model-serving layer because that’s what you’d do for an ML model in production. The assumption is that any use of AI needs the whole machinery of model management.

*   **What they actually need (Simpler Solution):** **Lean development, focus on the core application.** If you’re not actually training models (or only doing a one-off fine-tune), you likely don’t need most of the classic ML pipeline components. Tracking prompt changes can be as simple as using version control (commit messages can capture what you changed and why). If you’re collaborating with a small team, a shared document or simple spreadsheet might suffice to compare prompt variants and results. For deployment, if you’re calling an external API (like OpenAI), the “model serving” is already handled by the provider – you don’t need a Dockerized model server with an autoscaler. If you fine-tune a model, use the provider’s service (OpenAI’s fine-tuning API, Hugging Face hub, etc.) rather than hosting it yourself, at least initially. Only introduce tools like MLflow when you have multiple models, frequent experiments that need formal tracking, or a need to hand off between team members in a reproducible way. A good rule is: **don’t build pipeline infrastructure until you’ve found product-market fit and have a model that needs regular iteration or retraining.** In early stages, you can re-run scripts manually for training or data processing; that’s fine. The goal is to validate the idea, not to have a perfect pipeline.

*   **Why the complex choice hurts:** MLOps tools can be **time-consuming to set up and maintain**. For instance, deploying Kubeflow is a project in itself – it’s essentially “Kubernetes plus a bunch of ML-specific controllers,” which we already established is overkill for small teams. MLflow requires setting up tracking servers or fiddling with local file logging and tends to introduce version compatibility issues with its clients. These tools shine when you have dozens of models, multiple people tweaking experiments, nightly retraining jobs, etc. If you don’t have those, they become extra work with no payoff. You spend time writing YAML specs for pipelines or crafting entry points for MLflow, instead of improving the product. Moreover, enforcing heavy process too early can slow down iteration: for example, insisting on a formal pipeline for every prompt change could reduce the agility you have in just trying things interactively in a notebook or script. The result is high ceremony, low benefit. There’s also an element of misapplying technology: a prompt is not a model – it doesn’t have to be treated with the full lifecycle of code->train->validate->deploy in the same way as a custom ML model. As one industry expert quipped, *“The most expensive mistake a startup can make with AI is fine-tuning (or building out MLOps) too early.”* All the tracking, logging, and automated pipelines in the world won’t save a product that hasn’t found traction or an ML feature that isn’t actually useful yet. It’s better to focus on fast feedback and only layer in MLOps when the manual process truly becomes a bottleneck. [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/lukefryer4/fine-tuning-vs-prompt-engineering-a-cost-benefit-analysis-for-startups-3pmn)

*   **Technologies to Avoid at This Scale:** Avoid setting up **Kubeflow, MLflow, SageMaker Studio**, **Airflow** or **Prefect** flows for your LLM app’s initial version. Don’t integrate a full **CI/CD pipeline for model training** if you don’t actually have regular retraining needs. Avoid using a **feature store** (covered next) or data versioning systems like **DVC** for one-off datasets that aren’t rapidly changing. Essentially, don’t copy-paste the “MLOps stack” from a medium-to-large company into your 5k-line project. Also, be wary of new “LLMOps” platforms that promise to handle prompts/experiments – many are in early stages and will add complexity and constraints.

*   **Technologies Recommended Instead:** **Keep it simple and manual**. If you’re doing prompt engineering, use a Jupyter notebook or simple scripts to iterate. Maintain a changelog of what you’ve tried – could be as informal as a Markdown file or using Git history. If you are training/fine-tuning models occasionally, use managed services or simple scripts. For example, use **OpenAI’s fine-tuning API** or **AutoML** services where available, which handle a lot of ops for you. If you want easy experiment tracking without heavy setup, lightweight SaaS like **Weights & Biases** (which is basically one `pip install` and a few lines to log metrics) can be a middle ground – but even that, justify its use by real needs (e.g., dozens of experiments to compare). For scheduling simple jobs (data refresh, etc.), use **cron jobs or GitHub Actions** instead of a whole pipeline orchestrator. In short, favor approaches that require little to no maintenance. You can always add MLflow or Kubeflow later if you find yourself truly needing features like model versioning, but many small AI projects never reach that point.

### 7. **Feature Store Frenzy** – *Adding a feature store for a single model or trivial data*

*   **When it appears (Trigger):** You have an application with some data and maybe one ML model or LLM, and you recall that in proper ML architectures, there’s often a *feature store* to manage input data to models. Perhaps your use-case involves computing some features from raw data to feed into an LLM or classifier (e.g., computing user statistics, or summarizing recent user activity as input to a prompt), and you worry about consistency or freshness of that data. Influenced by talks or documentation from Uber (Michelangelo), Feast, or Tecton, you start believing you need a formal feature store to avoid “feature drift” or to serve features in real-time.

*   **What the developer thinks they need (Overengineered Choice):** Deploying or integrating a **Feature Store system** such as **Feast, Tecton, Azure Feature Store, Amazon SageMaker Feature Store**, etc. In practice, this might mean standing up additional infrastructure: e.g., a Redis or DynamoDB for online feature storage, a pipeline to compute features and materialize them to the store, versioning of features, feature definitions in a central catalog, and so on. The developer’s rationale is to ensure that features are consistent between model training and inference (even if they might not be doing much training) and to have low-latency retrieval of features for each request. They might invest time writing feature pipelines (maybe in Spark or Pandas), setting up the feature store’s database and API, and integrating the feature store client into their application.

*   **What they actually need (Simpler Solution):** **Calculate or retrieve features on the fly, or use your existing database.** For a small scale system, especially one where you’re mostly doing inference or using an external LLM, a feature store is rarely necessary. If you have a few numeric or categorical features you need to provide to a model or prompt, you can usually compute them when needed (just-in-time in code), cache them in memory if they’re expensive to compute, or pre-compute and store them in a normal database table. The classic motivations for feature stores are (A) multiple different ML models needing the same data in sync, (B) very fast online prediction needs, and (C) large teams collaborating on features. If you don’t squarely have those, don’t add the complexity. For example, if you have a recommendation model that needs a user’s last 10 interactions, you can fetch that from a database when needed or keep it in a simple cache – no need for a specialized system. If you have a nightly batch job that scores users, you can just write the results to your database. The **rule of one**: if you have only one or two models, and one small team, you can manage features with basic tooling. Embrace simplicity: Pandas in a scheduled job, or even a SQL script, can populate what you need for a long time. You can also leverage **infrastructure you already use** – for example, if your data lives in a SQL DB or a data warehouse (BigQuery, Snowflake), you can query it directly at inference time or use materialized views. [\[lavapi.com\]](https://www.lavapi.com/blog/feature-stores-production-when-to-use), [\[lavapi.com\]](https://www.lavapi.com/blog/feature-stores-production-when-to-use)

*   **Why the complex choice hurts:** Setting up a feature store adds yet another moving part – one that needs infrastructure and maintenance. For a solo dev, that’s a lot of extra operational weight to carry. Feature stores also tend to have specific formats and constraints, so you’ll spend time figuring out how to transform your data into that system, debug ingestion pipelines, and handle versioning of data. If something goes wrong with the features, you now have to debug not only your code but also the feature store pipeline. Meanwhile, if you had just used a simple join or query in your existing database, you could rely on known tools and skills. Additionally, many feature store benefits don’t manifest at small scale: for example, ensuring consistency between training and serving isn’t an issue if you only retrain rarely and can just reuse the same code or data files. A blog on feature stores notes that for small teams with a few models, a feature store “**adds overhead without benefit**… you probably don’t need it yet,” suggesting to stick with simple scripts or SQL until you truly have scale. The complexity of an additional system outweighs the marginal convenience it provides. It can also introduce latency if misconfigured – a poorly set up feature store could actually slow down feature access compared to just reading from a fast cache or database. The bottom line: if your operation is not at the scale of Uber or Facebook, you likely don’t have the problems that feature stores solve, and introducing one too early just creates new problems (data sync issues, system bugs, scaling the feature store itself, etc.). [\[lavapi.com\]](https://www.lavapi.com/blog/feature-stores-production-when-to-use), [\[lavapi.com\]](https://www.lavapi.com/blog/feature-stores-production-when-to-use)

*   **Technologies to Avoid at This Scale:** Do not spin up open-source **Feast** with a Redis online store for a project where a handful of SQL queries would suffice. Avoid using **Tecton** or **AWS SageMaker Feature Store** when you don’t have a large ML team and multiple production models. Don’t jump into **enterprise feature platforms (Databricks Feature Store, Google Feast on GCP)** unless you are hitting clear limitations with basic approaches. Essentially, any system whose primary pitch is “shared features at scale” should be viewed skeptically for a small app.

*   **Technologies Recommended Instead:** **Use your primary database or simple code.** If you have user or content data in a relational DB, add columns or tables for any features you need to compute, and update them via a scheduled script or trigger. If low latency is needed for a couple of values, and computation is heavy, consider an in-memory cache (even something like a Python `lru_cache` or a singleton dictionary in your app). For example, if you need a user’s last login or purchase history as a feature for an LLM prompt, just query your users table or a dedicated “user\_stats” table. If you want to pre-aggregate something daily, run a scheduled batch job (could be a simple cron job or a lightweight use of Cloud Functions) that writes to your DB. Embrace straightforward solutions: SQL views, CSV files, or even just computing on the fly if it’s not too slow – modern databases are quite fast, and you can often get away with real-time queries rather than caching, until volume grows. In summary, **prefer simple data pipelines** (maybe a script or a dbt job) and avoid introducing new systems until you truly need them.

### 8. **Telemetry Overload** – *Triple-pillar observability for a tiny application*

*   **When it appears (Trigger):** You’ve built a small AI-powered app (perhaps a Flask/FastAPI service calling an LLM and responding to a frontend). You want to ensure it’s reliable and you can troubleshoot issues, and you recall the “three pillars of observability” – logs, metrics, and traces – plus all the modern tooling around it (distributed tracing, aggregated logs, dashboards, alerts). Perhaps you or your team have used **Datadog, NewRelic, OpenTelemetry (OTel)**, etc., and you aim to instrument the new app with everything: custom metrics for every function, full trace spans for each request, log shipping to ELK or CloudWatch, dashboards for latency of each part of your pipeline, and so on. In short, you’re treating it like a production system at Google scale.

*   **What the developer thinks they need (Overengineered Choice):** A **comprehensive observability stack from day one.** This could mean instrumenting the code with OpenTelemetry SDK to trace each step of the request (so you can see precisely how long the LLM call vs DB call vs vector search takes), setting up a Prometheus/Grafana or DataDog to scrape metrics and display dashboards, and shipping all logs to a central system with search (like ELK stack or Splunk). The developer might also apply enterprise-grade log formatting, correlation IDs across services (even if there’s just one service now), and so on. The belief is that this will catch issues early and ensure the app is robust, or simply following a perceived best practice that *“all production systems must have full observability.”*

*   **What they actually need (Simpler Solution):** **Start with basic monitoring appropriate to your app’s complexity.** If you have a single service, you often can get very far with just good structured **logs** and a few key **metrics**. For instance, log each request’s outcome including important context (user ID, request type, maybe prompt or model ID) – kept on disk or a basic log service – and set up a simple **alert on errors** (even an email if error rate goes high). For metrics, maybe track overall request rate and latency (many PaaS give you this out of the box, or a small library can emit counters). You likely don’t need full **distributed tracing** unless you have a distributed system or complex asynchronous flows. A good approach is incremental: as one small-team observability guide notes, start with metrics for high-level health, add structured logs for debugging, and only add tracing if and when you truly have a multi-service or hard-to-follow flow. Many issues in a simple app can be debugged with logs and reading stack traces; you might not need a Jaeger/Zipkin view of a single request when you can just run the service locally to reproduce or add print statements. If using a cloud platform, leverage any **built-in monitoring** (AWS CloudWatch, etc.) rather than building your own stack. The principle is: **observe only what you need to make decisions**. If nobody is actively looking at 12 fancy dashboards, it’s not worth having them. A small app with a handful of users doesn’t generate enough unknown failure patterns to justify complex instrumentation. Start simple, and if you encounter a production incident that simpler logging couldn’t debug, *then* consider adding more tooling targeted at that gap. [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces), [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces)

*   **Why the complex choice hurts:** Maintaining a full observability stack is essentially like running yet another product – it has cost and complexity. Aggregating logs, metrics, and traces means setting up servers or services for each (or paying for expensive SaaS tiers). You also create **noise**: small teams often drown in data they don’t actually use. One expert observed that teams who “collect all three \[pillars] too early” end up with *“storage bills \[that] rise, dashboards multiply, and nobody knows which signal should answer which question.”* In other words, you generate lots of charts and data but not necessarily insight – and it can waste time sifting through it. Additionally, instrumenting everything (especially tracing) can add performance overhead to your app – you’re literally doing extra work on every request to record spans or metrics that might not be needed. And there’s a cognitive cost: every team member (if it’s not just you) now has to know how to use Kibana, Grafana, or whatever tools, which might be overkill for debugging a simple bug. It’s also easy to misconfigure these tools, leading to either missing important info or getting overwhelmed. Observability should follow architecture complexity; if you have one service and one developer, a lot of the complex setups provide little ROI. The Raff Labs guide on small-team observability puts it well: *“Most small teams should start with metrics and structured logs before investing deeply in tracing… don’t build the observability stack you think advanced teams are supposed to have. Build the one your current architecture can actually benefit from.”*. In short, overdoing telemetry can become a distraction and resource sink instead of a help. [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces) [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces), [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces)

*   **Technologies to Avoid at This Scale:** Avoid introducing **OpenTelemetry with full distributed tracing** for a single-service app; if you do use OTel, maybe limit it to metrics collection at first rather than spans. Avoid maintaining your own **ELK (Elasticsearch-Logstash-Kibana) stack** or **Prometheus/Grafana** stack for a tiny deployment – if you truly need these, consider using a hosted service to avoid the ops work. Don’t integrate the likes of **Jaeger** or **Zipkin** (tracing systems) if your requests basically go from a user to your service and back, without complex fan-out. Don’t obsess over collecting every metric (e.g., how many milliseconds each database query took) before you even have users – start with a few that matter (throughput, latency, error count). Also be cautious with enabling super-detailed debug logging in production for everything – that can be extremely noisy and expensive; log at info or warn on relevant events, not every step.

*   **Technologies Recommended Instead:** **Logging**: ensure you have your application logs being saved (even if just to a file or console that your PaaS captures). Use structured logs or at least consistent formats – it makes them far more useful. For metrics, many cloud or PaaS solutions automatically give you basic CPU/memory and request metrics. If not, a simple library (like Python’s `prometheus_client` or a StatsD client) can emit a few critical metrics to a lightweight backend. If you want alerting without fuss, services like **UptimeRobot or health checks** can monitor basic availability. Some simpler alternatives: use **Sentry** or a similar error tracking service to catch exceptions (this is often very low effort to set up and high value when something goes wrong). If on a tight budget or just developing, you can often get by with just tailing logs and ad-hoc prints. As your system grows, add only what addresses pains you’ve actually experienced: e.g., if a bug took too long to find because you lacked a metric, add that one metric. Adopt observability gradually in line with your needs, and prefer **managed services or simple SaaS** (Datadog, etc., have free tiers) over running complex infrastructure yourself as a lone dev. [\[rafftechnologies.com\]](https://rafftechnologies.com/learn/guides/observability-small-teams-metrics-logs-traces)

### 9. **Workflow Orchestra Overkill** – *Using heavy workflow engines for simple sequences*

*   **When it appears (Trigger):** Your application has some background tasks or multi-step processes. For example, maybe you need to nightly ingest new documents and then update embeddings, or a user request triggers a series of steps (like fetch data, then call an LLM, then email a report). As an experienced engineer, you recall solutions like **Apache Airflow, Temporal, Prefect, Step Functions** or other orchestration engines that ensure tasks run reliably. Influenced by the idea of “resilience” and seeing these tools in enterprise, you consider implementing one to manage your app’s workflows. Maybe an AI coding assistant even generated a Temporal workflow for you. The context is a relatively simple set of tasks, but you feel that to be robust or scalable, an advanced orchestrator is required.

*   **What the developer thinks they need (Overengineered Choice):** A **distributed workflow orchestration system** to handle task scheduling, retries, and coordination. This could be deploying **Temporal** (an open-source workflow engine from Uber) or using **Cadence, Apache Airflow, Luigi, Argo Workflows**, or cloud equivalents like **AWS Step Functions** for just a couple of tasks. The developer sets up workers, task queues, and writes workflows as if they might have dozens of parallel tasks – even if in reality it’s one or two sequential steps. The reasoning might be to get automatic retries, to separate concerns (each task in its own service), or simply because these systems guarantee state management (e.g., “Temporal will ensure my tasks complete even if the process crashes”). Essentially, using a sledgehammer (industrial workflow engine) for a small nail (a simple job or two).

*   **What they actually need (Simpler Solution):** **A simple scheduling or function call approach.** If the tasks are part of a request/response flow, just code the sequence in your app – call one function after the other (synchronously or asynchronously as appropriate). If the tasks are long-running or need to be decoupled, you can use a very basic queue or schedule:
    *   For periodic jobs (like daily or hourly tasks), a **cron job** (Unix cron, or a schedule in your cloud provider) is often sufficient.
    *   For asynchronous background jobs triggered by user action, many web frameworks have simple background task frameworks or you can use a minimal queue like **Redis Queue (RQ)** or **Celery** (which can be as lightweight as a single Python process for all tasks).
    *   Even simpler, for Python and JavaScript, you could use **threading or background workers** within the same app process for light tasks.
        The idea is to leverage simplicity. If a task fails, you can catch exceptions and implement a retry with backoff in a few lines of code, or rely on a basic queue that has a retry mechanism. Write a script for nightly maintenance tasks and use the OS scheduler. These approaches cover a lot of ground without introducing a large new dependency. Only if you truly have complex dependencies or a high volume of batch processes (hundreds of different jobs with schedules, etc.) might a more robust solution be justified. For many internal AI workflows (like RAG data updates or re-training triggers), a basic approach is enough until you scale up.

*   **Why the complex choice hurts:** Enterprise workflow engines are complex **by design** – they solve hard problems that you likely don’t have at small scale (like coordinating hundreds of workers, surviving many failures seamlessly, versioning workflows, etc.). By using them, you inherit that complexity. Temporal, for instance, requires running a cluster of services (or using their cloud offering, which costs money) and has its own programming model (you write workflow functions that have to be deterministic, you manage activity workers, etc.). This is a significant learning curve and maintenance burden for one developer. Airflow is notorious for requiring a bunch of infrastructure (databases, schedulers, web UI) and can be finicky to manage; it’s fantastic for a team of data engineers running 50 ETL jobs, but total overkill for an app that maybe has one nightly job. Misusing these can also cause more issues: e.g., if not configured properly, you might spend time debugging *why* your Temporal workflow hung or why your Airflow task didn’t trigger – issues that wouldn’t exist in a simple script. Additionally, writing and testing workflows in these frameworks can be slower than just coding the logic: you often have to run the whole engine to test, whereas a plain function you can run in a debugger. As a solo dev, every new system is “another hat to wear” – here you’re becoming the DevOps for a workflow engine *and* the developer. The benefit (say, automatic retries) could be achieved with a 5-line `try/except` loop in many cases. As the Beam blog on multi-agent orchestration (cited earlier) might rephrase: start with the simplest approach, add complexity only when necessary – often, just writing straightforward code is the simplest approach for orchestrating a few tasks. In summary, using heavy orchestration too early is investing a lot of effort for theoretical reliability or scalability that your current usage probably doesn’t need. [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/nebulagg/multi-agent-orchestration-a-guide-to-patterns-that-work-1h81)

*   **Technologies to Avoid at This Scale:** Avoid deploying **Temporal/Cadence** servers, or adding a dependency on a heavy cloud service like **AWS Step Functions**, for very minimal workflow needs. Don’t reach for **Apache Airflow** just to run a daily data update or to coordinate an LLM call and an email; likewise avoid requiring users to install heavy libraries or services for orchestration (e.g., telling your colleagues “first set up a Kafka and Temporal cluster to run our QA bot” is a red flag). If you find yourself writing YAML/JSON for a state machine that has only a few steps, reconsider. Also, be cautious with emerging “agent orchestration” platforms that promise to manage LLM tool sequences – many of those are early-stage and may add more chaos than value.

*   **Technologies Recommended Instead:** Use language-native scheduling or minimal job queues. Examples: Python’s `schedule` library or simply cron for periodic jobs; **Celery/RQ** for background jobs (they require Redis but that’s less overhead than a full workflow engine and is commonly used anyway). If you’re on a cloud, use their simplest offerings: e.g., **CloudWatch/EventBridge + Lambda** for scheduled tasks on AWS, or GitHub Actions for nightly jobs like data scrapes or model retraining – these are managed and remove the need to run your own scheduler. Even a half-hour effort to set up a cron job on your server can replace a massive amount of orchestrator complexity. If your workflow is user-triggered and must be asynchronous (like the user triggers something that might take 5 minutes), consider in-process solutions (threads, asyncio tasks) or a basic queue consumer. The goal: achieve reliability with minimal components. Should a task fail mid-run, you can often get by with writing to a “checkpoint” (file or DB) and having another simple script to resume or retry it. Save the fancy orchestration for when you truly have many concurrent, complex processes that justify it.

***

**In conclusion**, as a solo developer or small team building AI-driven applications, **resist the urge to import the entire kitchen sink of enterprise tech.** Successful startups and hack projects alike thrive on **focus and speed**, which often means doing the simplest thing that works. Each of the anti-patterns above boils down to a common theme: **Don’t let “what if we succeed massively” or “that’s what big companies do” drive your early technical decisions.** Build for the scale and complexity you have **now**, not for a hypothetical future. Use battle-tested simple tools and add complexity only when you have concrete evidence that you need it. This will keep your AI project nimble, easier to understand, and far easier to pivot or debug. As Martin Fowler’s classic advice goes (and it applies beyond microservices): “**don’t start with \[the complex solution] even if you’re sure you’ll need it later**”. In the rapidly evolving AI landscape of 2024–2026, agility is a superpower – and nothing kills agility faster than unnecessary entanglement with overly complex infrastructure. Keep it simple, prove the value of your idea, and only then scale up the sophistication as a conscious choice rather than a default. [\[martinfowler.com\]](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/MonolithFirst.html)
